data.title: CIO Notebook: U.S. Non-Farm Payrolls Disappoint, All Signs Point To Fed Cuts
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

The probability of a Fed rate cut in September now stands at 100% and the potential for an outsized 50bps cut before year-end has increased as concerns about labor market deterioration outweigh the threat of higher prices from tariffs.

August U.S. non-farm payrolls came in light for the month, posting a gain of only +22k versus the consensus for +75k. The report was highly anticipated, coming on the heels of a significant miss in July coupled with meaningful downward revisions, both of which contributed to the ouster of the head of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, Dr. Erika McEntarfer, by President Donald Trump. Revisions were once again negative in aggregate, with the June print revised downward by -27k, resulting in a -13k print, while July’s number benefited from a positive revision of +6k. These changes, combined with today’s lackluster report, pushed the three-month trailing average for payroll gains to only +29k, reminiscent of 2020 levels.

Looking through the drivers of this month’s gains, health care and social assistance hiring combined for +47k, well off the prior month’s total, while leisure and hospitality rebounded to post a +38k gain. Retail was also higher by +11k. Federal government employment continues to decline, down another -15k in the month of August and off by -97k from its peak in January. Mining, manufacturing, and wholesale trade all posted losses for the month as well. As expected, the unemployment rate rose to 4.3%, driven in part by a welcome increase in the participation rate from 62.2% to 62.3%. The number of employed persons also rose by +288k in the month, as the labor force grew by +436k. Average hourly wages also moved higher by +0.3% month-over-month and +3.7% year-over-year, helping to dampen tariff impacts for consumers; wages were higher even as hours worked declined slightly.

We believe that two takeaways from this release are worth watching in the coming months. First, the U.S. economy continues to experience job losses in goods producing industries. The intent of tariffs, as understood by most investors, is two-fold: to increase revenue and to encourage a more level playing field for U.S. goods, which would in turn incentivize an increase in goods production in the U.S. In August, goods producing jobs in the U.S. were down by -25k in aggregate, and more specifically in manufacturing, -12k jobs were lost last month, and -78k have been lost this year. While we believe there are tailwinds in the form of tax incentives, a lower interest rate environment, and policy changes for companies to increase capital expenditure and shift more production to the U.S., there has been little to show for it in the labor market up to this point.

When looking more closely at the data, the second is the trend lower in hiring in health care, social assistance, and the U.S. government. In the post-pandemic period, these three industries accounted for the bulk of the jobs added. Should a deceleration in job gains in these sectors continue, private payrolls in areas such as financial services, technology, and the goods producing sectors mentioned above would need to accelerate to fill the gap. With the potential for productivity benefits derived from AI on the horizon, perhaps translating to the slow pace of entry level hiring in professional services, it might prove difficult to get that needed boost.

Translating the data into likely action, the probability of a Fed cut at the September meeting now stands at 100%. In addition, an outsized cut of 50 basis points between now and the end of the year has increased as well, as concerns about a more meaningful deterioration in the labor market are more than offsetting the threat of higher prices due to tariffs – particular as the fate of the Liberation Day reciprocal tariffs hangs in the judicial balance. Equity markets appear to be discounting the risk of a broader economic slowdown, instead moving higher on the prospects for lower interest rates; small caps and growth are both benefiting in the early session. Treasury yields are lower across the curve, shifting in parallel with the expectations for a decline in the front end.

While we acknowledge that the risks appear to be somewhat less balanced after the last two non-farm payrolls reports, we remain of the view that the Fed will cut twice this year and will likely continue to cut rates for a cumulative 100 basis points through the first half of 2026. For fixed income investors, we remain focused on repositioning cash into more attractive opportunities and encourage a thoughtful approach to both duration and credit quality in this dynamic environment. Within equities, we believe that a rotation is likely to continue through the remainder of 2026 and advise allocations in small and non-US equities to complement existing U.S. large cap exposure.

Important Information:

This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types. Diversification does not guarantee profit or protect against loss in declining markets. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Unless otherwise indicated, returns shown reflect reinvestment of dividends and distributions. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Portfolio positioning views expressed herein are those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group, which may include those of the Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee. Asset allocation and positioning views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. The Private Wealth Investment Group works in partnership with the Office of the CIO. The Private Wealth Investment Group also consults regularly with portfolio managers and investment officers across the firm. The Asset Allocation Committee is comprised of professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates. Asset Allocation Committee members are polled on asset classes and the positional views are representative of an Asset Allocation Committee consensus. The views of the Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole and Neuberger Berman advisers and portfolio managers may take contrary positions to the views of the Asset Allocation Committee or the Private Wealth Investment Group. The Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group views do not constitute a prediction or projection of future events or future market behavior. Defensive positioning generally means an underweight bias on allocations to risk assets such as equities and alternatives. Positioning views may change over time without notice and actual client positioning may vary significantly. Discussion of yield characteristics or total returns of different asset classes are for illustrative purposes only. Such asset classes, such as equities and fixed income, may have significantly different overall risk-return characteristics which should be consider before investing.

The information in this material may contain projections, market outlooks or other forward-looking statements regarding future events, including economic, asset class and market outlooks or expectations, and is only current as of the date indicated. data.title: Mueller Water Products: Upside Is Still Limited
data.description:
Back in May of this year, one company that I decided to dig into was Mueller Water Products (MWA). If you're not familiar with the business, it focuses on providing customers with water flow and water management solutions. I have always found the water industry to be incredibly interesting because of scarcity concerns, and the fact that all life as we know it requires water in some capacity. Unfortunately, even though the business was continuing to achieve growth, I argued that the stock was frankly too expensive for my liking. This caused me to call it a ‘hold’ candidate, which is what I rate a company that I expect will perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future.

Since that time, the stock has risen, but only by 6.3%. That's pretty close to the 7.7% rise that the S&P 500 has seen over the same window of time. The good news is that management expects growth to continue throughout the rest of the 2025 fiscal year. Overall leverage for the company is incredibly low. And relative to other similar firms, the stock is certainly trading slightly on the cheap side of the spectrum. Unfortunately, because of its absolute valuation, I cannot in good faith upgrade it just yet.

Taking a fresh look at Mueller Water Products

The most recent data that we have regarding Mueller Water Products covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $380.3 million. That's an increase of 6.6% compared to the $356.7 million the company reported one year earlier. But to really understand this increase, we should look at both of its operating segments. The first of these is its Water Flow Solutions business, which focuses on the production and sale of iron gate valves, specialty valves, and service brass products. And the second of these is its Waste Management Solutions segment, which consists of its product and service portfolio associated with fire hydrants, repair and installation services, natural gas products, metering products, leak detection products, crush your management and control products, and related solutions.

For the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for this segment came in at $216.6 million. That happens to be 4.1% above the $208.1 million the company reported the previous year. According to management, this was thanks to increased volumes of iron gate and specialty valves, on top of higher pricing across most of its product lines. Unfortunately, service brass product volumes declined. In the third quarter earnings call, management didn't really provide much in the way of detail here. The only thing they did talk about involved the decline in brass volumes, which was mainly because of backlog normalization and inventory destocking from its customers and throughout its channel. Apparently, in the previous year, shipments had been elevated because of higher backlog. So what we are seeing here is a return to more normal levels. The fact that overall revenue for this segment still managed to increase during this time is certainly promising.

When it comes to the Water Management Solutions business, revenue growth was even more impressive, with sales shooting up 10.2% from $148.6 million to $163.7 million. Increased volumes of repair products and hydrants did the heavy lifting here, though higher pricing certainly played a role. Unfortunately, volumes associated with natural gas distribution products happened to counteract some of the strong growth. Just like when it came to the brass products under the Water Flow Solutions business, the natural gas distribution products volumes declined because of inventory normalization.

With revenue for the company rising, it should come as no surprise that profits would be largely higher. Net income grew from $47.3 million to $52.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the rise was from $49.5 million to $53.2 million. This is not to say that every profitability metric for the company improved. Unfortunately, operating cash flow contracted from $87.3 million to $67.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a more modest decline from $63 million to $60.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business ticked up modestly from $85.2 million to $86.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. As the previous charts throughout this article that looked at the segment-by-segment data illustrated, this increase was thanks to growth across both operating segments. And that growth brought with it higher segment profits that resulted in higher overall profits and, for the most part, cash flows. When it comes to the year in its entirety, we can expect overall revenue of between $1.41 billion and $1.42 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean an increase of 7.2%. On the bottom line, management expects EBITDA of between $318 million and $322 million. Even at the low end, that would represent a nice increase over the $284.7 million reported for 2024. And based on the data provided by management, operating cash flow should be around $210 million, while adjusted net profits are guiding toward $198.8 million.

All of these represent improvements over what the company saw last year. And what's really interesting here is that this is in spite of the fact that management does anticipate some impact caused by tariffs. Excluding potential copper-related tariffs, the company said that it expects an annualized impact from tariffs amounting to between 3% and 4% of its cost sales. This is actually lower than what was previously anticipated, with management chalking the improvement up to lower China related tariffs and certain supply chain initiatives that the management team at the business has been pushing for. To offset these, the firm has said that it will push through price increases. This includes price increases on specialty valves and repair products. The impact of those should occur in the final quarter of this year.

Another thing that seems to be helping the company would be certain strategic reorganization moves that management has been making. The company incurred decommissioning and demolition costs associated with its legacy foundry in Illinois. This included $5.1 million of strategic reorganization and other charges. The benefits from that closure should start to show up in fourth quarter results. On top of this, management is continuing to make certain investments that should take place throughout next year and beyond. This includes investments aimed at achieving operational improvements at its facilities and with its employees, as well as making certain capital investments of an unknown nature at this time. We don't know what capital expenditures will be for next year. But it's unlikely that they would be much different from the $50 million to $52 million expected for 2025.

If we use the estimates for 2025 and the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of Mueller Water Products are currently priced. These multiples are certainly not bad, but as a value investor, I want to see something cheaper than this. The good news is that, at least relative to other similar firms, the stock is not expensive. In the table below, I compared it to five such businesses. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than our candidate. Three of the five ended up being cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while two of the five were cheaper when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. So this places the company a little toward the lower end of the spectrum compared to other similar enterprises.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Mueller Water Products 21.2 20.0 13.4
The Timken Company (TKR) 17.6 11.5 9.4
ESCO Technologies (ESE) 45.3 25.5 24.6
Enpro (NPO) 54.6 24.8 19.6
Hillenbrand (HI) 33.1 13.6 8.6
ATS Corporation (ATS) 38.7 17.4 27.1

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I do think that Mueller Water Products is a fascinating company. But honestly, it's just not cheap enough for my liking. Continued growth is encouraging, and it seems as though management will succeed in improving bottom-line results even more thanks to the aforementioned changes that the company has made. But until we see some evidence that the stock will become quite a bit cheaper than it is now, I cannot rate it any higher than a ‘hold.’

data.title: Petrobras: When Market Fear Creates Long-Term Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I look at Petrobras (PBR) (NYSE:PBR.A) I see a company that delivers solid results quarter after quarter, and a market that continues to value it with multiples of liquidation. This remains the same as in my previous article.

Q2 2025 was another proof of its resilience: net income reached R$26.7B (~$4.7B), adjusted EBITDA was R$52.3B (+5.1% QoQ), and production reached 2.91 MM boed (+7.8% YoY). However, ADR remains at $12.3, with a P/E FWD of 4.2x and an EV/EBITDA of 3.0x, levels that are more than 50% below global peers.

I know that the political noise is not going to go away, but I find it difficult to justify why a company with such high margins, growth in production and a solid balance sheet with net debt/EBITDA of 1.0x should continue trading at such depressed multiples.

In my view, the additional downside is limited because it is already discounted into the price, while the upside remains very wide. Therefore, my position is clear: Petrobras remains a buy for the long-term investor.

Q2 2025: production, margins and discipline

The results of Q2 2025 reflect a growing business with solid foundations. Production reached 2.91 MM boed, with a rise of 7.8% YoY, and oil production in Brazil reached 2.32 MM bpd, +7.6%YoY. Thus, we observe that pre-salt remains the engine of PBR’s growth, with ramp-ups of new platforms that keep costs competitive and margins high.

Exports, meanwhile, increased to 874k bpd, with China as the main destination (52%) and a significant growth in the US, which already represents 8% compared to 5% last year. I believe that this rebalancing of markets improves resilience to external shocks.

As for dividends, PBR approved regular payments of R$8.66B (~$1.6B), which equates to an effective dividend yield of more than 10%.

The company decided not to distribute extras, prioritizing CAPEX funding of $111B until 2029, a plan that seeks to consolidate its leadership in offshore and advance the energy transition.

Valuation: fundamentals vs. perception

The valuation of PBR today reflects more fear than reality. With a P/E FWD of 4.2x and an EV/EBITDA of 3.0x, the stock trades with crisis multiples, while Exxon (XOM) or TotalEnergies (TTE) do it between 4-7 times.

The paradox is that I observe that PBR combines solid cash generation, very high operating margins, and double-digit dividends, with a balance sheet that is healthier than that of many peers.

I would like to stress that it is not just about static valuation, but also about growth and profitability. In Q2, production rose by almost 8%, net margins remained at robust levels, and return on capital continues to show that the company is not only large but also efficient.

That combination of low multiples and higher growth is what makes the price gap unsustainable in the long run.

Scenario analysis: pricing Petrobras' upside

With PBR around $12.3, the valuation scenarios show me that the additional downside is low and the upside significant.

In a pessimistic scenario, with Brent at $65 and multiples of 3.5x EV/EBITDA, the stock would be estimated to be at $11-12, implying that the current price already reflects much of that shock.

In the base scenario, with Brent at $75-80 and multiples of 6.0x, I estimate that the ADR should be quoted around $17-18, a 50-70% jump from current levels.

Finally, in the optimistic scenario, with Brent above $85 and less political noise, I do not rule out a value of $20-21, which would mean a convergence towards multiples closer to those of their international peers.

Conclusion - Buy The Discount

Q2 2025 made it clear that Petrobras combines production growth, high margins, and dividend discipline. With an ADR around $12.3, the market continues to value it with multiples of liquidation, even though its fundamentals show a solid company, profitable and able to finance one of the most ambitious investment plans in the sector.

My reading is simple: the downside is limited because much of the risk is already built into the price, while the upside is too attractive. PBR is still a buy.

Political noise will continue to generate volatility in the short term, as is the case in Latin America in general and Brazil in particular, but in the long term, the real value of the company will prevail, and multiples will tend to converge towards global industry levels.

data.title: Tails We Win, Heads You Lose': How The U.S. Has Managed To Avoid A Recession, For Now
data.description:
According to a new report from TD Economics, the U.S. has been an economic disruptor of itself. Whether it be to its own business cost structures and trade flows with tariff policies, or labor force dynamics with uncertainty and immigration policies. Even so, Beata Caranci, Chief Economist at TD, says the U.S. is paying a lower economic cost for those policies compared to other countries, which she describes as a ‘tails we win, heads you lose’ scenario.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - Our next guest says that the US has become a disruptor of itself in everything from cost structures to trade flow to immigration. And yet, while many economists expected inflation and recession to hit pretty quickly, it really hasn't materialized as expected. But there still is a way to go. And yes, Canada is along for the ride.

Beata Caranci is Chief Economist at TD, and she joins me now. Great report that you just put out and two reports we're going to talk about. And I want to start by asking you about the title, "Tails, We Win. Heads, You Lose." I don't like playing those games, Beata. And you're talking about the US economy. Why the title?

Beata Caranci - Yeah. I think because they've put in place the greatest amount of disruption to their labor market institutions and everything you mentioned off the top, and yet it's still an economy that's outperforming the peers, who are really only absorbing the disruption on the trade side. They're not dealing with all the other aspects that are happening in the US.

So it really does speak to the construct of the economy. A lot of their growth drivers come off of investment and innovation. And that's where Trump has been quite supportive in policies and further deregulation in the future.

The consumer has not done as well in the US economy. It depends which pockets you want to look at. But certainly, I think it's being described as resilient. But resilient does not mean strong. So it's definitely absorbing an impact from everything that's happening, but much better than many people had anticipated at this stage.

Kim Parlee - I want to give credit where credit's due. I have the benefit of talking to you over many periods of time. And the last time -- the last few times, I know you were not one of those who had a recession call for the United States, you and your team. And that's not what we've seen so far. But again, you're highlighting here the difference between resilience and strength. And are we starting to see things to move in that direction now?

Beata Caranci - Yeah. So, I think, when you look at what's happening in the job market, or what's happened in the first six months of the year, seven months of the year, we had a restatement of the jobs data through a massive revision as one of the largest we've had in history outside of the pandemic.

Kim Parlee - To quote you, "job market revisions shatter resiliency narrative."

Beata Caranci - Yeah. And so we thought, we had a little bit wrong. Because while we didn't forecast a recession, we still thought the job market would take a little bit of a hit.

And we had this narrative happening in the US, where the job market was incredibly strong. The revision wiped that narrative away. And what it told us is it's only about 35,000 jobs being created on a three-month basis. That number, for the size of that job market, is Canadian-like. So it's quite low for them. They're 10 times the size of the economy. We're seeing very low job churn, which means people are not quitting their jobs in search of other jobs, which is usually a sign that you don't have the confidence, or there's not something out there where you can skills match to a better degree and more pay. And firms are not hiring to a great degree either.

So we've seen deteriorations, not just in the job numbers, which are still advancing, so there's not job losses. The unemployment rate is still low. People filing for insurance claims is still low. But when you look at it holistically and pull the lens back, there's not a lot happening underneath it. It's just in this trudging-along mode. And that does tell us that there's an underlying weakness happening. Not recession-like, but certainly, many people are saying, if this were to continue for another six to eight months, it could push you into that lane.

Kim Parlee - You talk about in the report -- there's a great line here that we have to become "data dogs" and sniff every corner for information. And you kind of highlighted a few things in terms of the data, what it's showing on the labor front. What else are you looking at to understand, I guess, the true composition of what's going on below the surface?

Beata Caranci - Yeah. So the consumer is big. They're the majority of the US economy, and they are a byproduct. What you see on the consumption side is a byproduct of what's happening in the labor market. And we dissected that one a little bit more in our report.

We're quite familiar with this narrative in Canada, that when you remove high population growth by changing your immigration policy, you remove a stimulus to the economy. In ours, we saw it through the housing market, but also through consumption, when people come in and they buy clothing and phones, and all sorts of things.

Kim Parlee - And houses.

Beata Caranci - Well, the US has taken out, at the border, in terms of how many people they've stopped from entering, about two million by the end of this year, we estimate. But more importantly, there's about 11 million undocumented workers who probably don't feel great about going out and spending and gathering and going to amusement parks and restaurants, because the ICE arrests are so elevated and rising. So there's an insecurity there.

So we estimate that those two channels are probably depressing consumer spending as well. That gets less and less as you go further out, like next year. Because you hit a lower level of population growth, but it stays at that lower level. It doesn't continue to decline. But right now, there is a big consumer impact happening from that channel as well.

And then we're looking at the inflation data. So there's a little bit of a disconnect in the narrative there. Many people are saying, well, when you look at aggregate inflation in the US, it is definitely better than many people had expected. That is because the service side of the economy has normalized. Remember, we used to have, like, costs at 8% on housing and other factors. That's come down quite a bit. However, the good side, which is a side that's being tariffed, is reaccelerating. And that's both on a three- and six-month basis.

So there's no denying there's some pass-through of prices happening. It's just happening in a very narrow portion of the economy right now. So if that continues to broaden, as we expect, the consumers are going to continue to feel it. So we're really focused on those job consumption variables. And then there's always the investment side that puts the counter argument.

Kim Parlee - You touch on this. You talked about the trade deals and how you talk about your language. They've morphed into the bigger strategy around investment deals.

Beata Caranci - They started off everybody thinking these are going to be tariff deals. That's part of it. But really, there's another intention, which is to get more investment into the US by commitments of procurements, direct investment of moving activity, or purchases of US exports.

And energy supply is a big one. Defense is a big one. Defense purchases. And so there's about $1.5 trillion of commitments already made by basically three regions - Korea, Japan, and the EU - the EU making the biggest.

And so even if you only see a portion of that come through, that is an impulse to growth. Many people wouldn't have that in their forecast. We don't have it in the forecast for the US because you have to see when those commitments come through.

They are basically commitments through the entirety of Trump's term, but you don't know how they will be staggered and to what degree. A little bit loosey goosey still. We don't know all the details about the timing from the countries themselves. So there's an element that is an additional stimulus factor, that many people wouldn't have thought would have come out of the tariff deals.

Kim Parlee - And does that mean the consumer, if they can tread water, that maybe the stimulus could come in and be a tailwind? Or are you still -- when you look further out, is it -- just lessens the blow?

Beata Caranci - Well, it's a timing issue. Because you have the tariffs being absorbed now, either by businesses, and a portion of it is getting passed on. A little bit more will continue to get passed on as the year goes on.

But then you have the One Big Beautiful Bill coming in next year, with a lot of tax incentives. And so when the consumer is getting a little bit depressed on the frontend in 2025, in 2026, you start to see offsets coming through. And so you may not see, on net, a big movement in consumer spending growth, because they start to cancel each other out, but you also don't get a worsening in the condition.

And so that's one of the reasons we don't have a recession call for the US, because there's a lot of moving parts in there. And if you just focus on the negative, you probably overestimate the downside.

Kim Parlee - The first report we talked about, "Tails, We Win. Heads, You Lose." The second report is, "Questions? We've Got Answers." I know there's a lot of questions out there right now. Given everything we just talked about in the US right now, how has Canada's economic outlook shifted over the last few months, let's say, a year, now that you're observing what's going on?

Beata Caranci - So Canada is an economy where I thought it would be in far worse situation than where we are today. So, like the US, it has a resilient nature around it, in terms of the story. Definitely not strong. Not as resilient as what you see in the US, because the trade side is really getting hammered.

But outside of that, we've seen incredible strength out of the consumer. And I think a lot of Canadians would be surprised to learn that the Canadian consumer, their spending growth has outpaced the US significantly.

Kim Parlee - Really?

Beata Caranci - By about 2.5 times.

Kim Parlee - 2.5 times?

Beata Caranci - Yeah, the growth. Not just last quarter, but in the last -- basically, the first six months of the year and even if you go back a full year beyond that. So we like to be humble about our approach. But in this particular area, the consumer is definitely holding up a good portion of the economy.

Now, the second quarter data that was really strong on consumer spending, was also the elbows-up quarter. So we'll see if that--

Kim Parlee - Buying my BC and Nova Scotia wine, and not the gin from wherever.

Beata Caranci - Yes. And vacationing, and all of those things happening domestically. So it works. You can see it in the data. But we'll see about the sustainability of it.

And part of the reason it works is because the job market, although it hasn't been as vibrant as it's been in the past, again, it's averaging about 15,000 to 18,000 jobs a month when you average it out over three months. So about half the pace of what we're seeing in the US, for an economy that's 1/10 the size. So that's what I mean, that it's not really firing on all cylinders, but certainly there's pockets of resiliency coming through.

The housing market is another one. We've seen several months now of sales coming up, a little bit of price resilience starting to come back in. So basically going back to our traditional Canadian drivers of housing and consumers being the tailwind to the economy, and then the weak spots are being exports and investment, opposite of the US story.

Investment is not strong at all. We don't show the propensity to spend in areas of intellectual property and technology and equipment, where that's a shining star in the US.

Kim Parlee - Well, the next question you have in your report, which, actually, I think just segues is, is that going to change? When you have Prime Minister Carney, the incoming government, who's been given the mandate essentially to nation build. Is that enough? Is that a big enough tailwind to really start to finally jumpstart that I think a lot of Canadians have been asking for a long time?

Beata Caranci - Well, it's not going to make it worse. How about we start there?

Kim Parlee - That's not very optimistic.

Beata Caranci - The good news is that -- so we have to manage our expectations. So you have C5. And that's intended to remove some of the barriers and speed up permitting processes on projects and other factors. We have the prime minister's office creating a list of priorities on projects that could be shovel ready.

Kim Parlee - Key personnel to shepherd them along.

Beata Caranci - Yeah. And we've heard of ports in Churchill and Montreal and other things that look like they can get moving earlier. All good news.

But the reality is that a lot of the information and control of these projects still falls within the domain of the provinces, and having consensus and agreement on projects and the movement of them, as well as we have to make sure we have Indigenous consultation processes that are proper.

And so as much as we would like to take an 18-year process and drop it into a 5- and 7-year format, it will remain to be seen how much of that we can excel at. It will be a big challenge. So we have to manage the expectations on the speed of projects. But certainly, the fact that there's lists being created, identified, prioritized. We have companies showing interest in it. This is all positive.

Carney himself has indicated that he wants to think through about $500 billion in capital projects over five years. That's 25% of GDP. He could do it. We would be in an economy that's unrecognizable, relative to where we are today.

Kim Parlee - He just wants to spend like Canadians in the second quarter.

Beata Caranci - That's right. But on the investment side. That's a huge, huge challenge. It's ambitious. But hey, why not go for it? Make it a moonshot.

Kim Parlee - And I guess the defense side, too, plays into this as well, in terms of the nation building. Because there's all the projects, the pipelines you mentioned too, but -- maybe in partnership with the US. I mean, maybe not. We're still waiting to hear. But defense spending is a big chunk.

Beata Caranci - So, defense spending, the long-term goal is to get it up to 5% of GDP. This year, we'll hopefully be at 2% of GDP. So there's a significant amount of spend there.

Part of it is going to be dedicated for wages and salaries. So better conditions for those serving in the military and veterans themselves. So that's--

Kim Parlee - Stimulative.

Beata Caranci - And then the other part is for the building of equipment and capital, and the technology and the cybersecurity, and all that comes with it.

Defense is an interesting area, because it has not been our stronghold. But when you look at other countries, like the US, it has a lot of positive spin-offs and other industries. And so, if done well, while you're thinking it's only defense spending, the technology you develop and use in that area can be used in other areas. The propensity for growth and the optimism around it is high, but it always comes down to execution.

Kim Parlee - Let's get back to some of the questions. Last two in the report, then I might, if I can time, I'll squeeze one more in there.

Given what you're seeing for Canada-- again, weakness. We've seen some real strength. But resilience, I'll say. And then it's starting to come down. What does the Bank of Canada do? Is the Bank of Canada done cutting rates?

Beata Caranci - It's quite possible. Because for them, and the way they defined the neutral rate, where they think it's all consistent with stable inflation and jobs, they're already in that range that they've defined.

We think there's room for at least two more cuts, one on the very minimum. That's what the market has priced in. But I don't think they'll do one and done. If they're going to do one, I always think -- what is this expression? Just like potato chips, you never just have one.

So I think there'll be a second coming. And the logic there is that we are going through an industrial rotation. You end up with capital destruction usually first, and then the creation side on a lag.

And so we know there's industries being pressured. We know there's uncertainty with the tariffs that is affecting business cost models. So, ideally, you should be running a lower interest rate or a cheaper cost of capital in that type of environment until you can get to the other side. Because you need to incent a greater amount of investment to backfill the stagnation or losses that might be happening in other areas.

So inflation is also being extremely cooperative. In the last inflation report, we're starting to see better numbers coming in for Canada. The government's removed many of the reciprocal tariffs on the US. So that'll lower prices, or help at least on that front.

And our economy is running in what we call a slack position. It's running below its potential space. So there's supply that's just sitting idle. So there's a number of factors that argue that if you cut a couple of more times, it's not going to stoke inflation to any great degree, but it could be that little bit of incentive to push drivers on investment, re-accelerate the housing market and other factors that help the economy recover.

Kim Parlee - And also, I guess, helping -- people think about the loonie. And I remember, I think people, one of the things we are preparing for is that the decades or the years of having a higher US dollar, lower loonie changing. This at least dampens that a little bit, if they keep cutting rates.

Beata Caranci - So it usually puts pressure on the loonie. And that usually helps you on the import side. Sorry, on the export side, because you look more competitive.

So that's a possibility. But I think right now, when you look at how the currency is being driven, it's not so much on the interest rate differentials. It's more on just the plain uncertainty, the risk environment that's happening, and that embedded notion of safety.

Kim Parlee - Interesting. Okay. Housing, I said we were going to get to it. So last question, is Canada's housing market showing signs of life? Is this a flash in the pan? Is there something more to it?

Beata Caranci - No, I think it's got some legs to it. Yeah. We've now had about four months of sales recovering, making a steady pace. So that's a trend for economists. Three is a trend, let alone four.

And it looks like what we may be seeing is, when everybody hit the pause button in that March, April, May period, when all the tariff uncertainty and the 51st state and everything was just an upheaval, everybody just hit, like, let's just see where this economy is going. Maybe feeling a little bit better for some segments of the economy.

If you're in non-trade sectors, the job market still actually looks pretty good from that perspective. And you're in a relatively low interest rate environment, relative to, let's say, the US. They have 30-year mortgage rates that are sitting over 6%. We're well below that in Canada.

So you're starting to see demand start to flow in. It doesn't mean that you get into this booming market. Obviously, we know, in places like the Toronto market, the condo market is very oversupplied. It takes time for that to come off. But I think this one has legs to it.

Kim Parlee - My last question that I didn't plan on asking you, but I'm going to ask you now. If you think about Canada -- we've only got about 90 seconds -- what are some of the opportunities that maybe Canada should be thinking about as we reimagine the economy?

Beata Caranci - So this gets a little bit controversial, I think, when I propose it. We have been looking at partners and expanding our trade relationships with the EU, and others, and shifting a little bit away from the US. And you can actually see that happening in the trade data as a share of our exports. There's more going ex-US. So that's positive.

We still don't have a repair on our relationship with China. And it would be peculiar to have -- the US is 20% of the global economy, Europe 20%, but then you ignore the other 20%. They are a very big consumer. And so there is an opportunity there for Canada to find some strategic partnerships, where it makes sense.

One of the areas we're thinking about is that, although Canada has protected its auto sector by putting on very high tariffs of EVs coming in from China - and that makes sense, because they're heavily subsidized in China - they are the most innovative in terms of their technology for batteries. And it would be a shame that we can't find a way to do a partnership, maybe in the battery production domestically in Canada.

So workers would be used in Canada. We already have all the critical minerals they need to produce their batteries. And because it's a market leader and we can do it at a lower cost, it might also jumpstart EV production, in general, in Canada, where we are -- there's no way we're going to hit our EV targets where we are today, in terms of adoption on the retail market. So there's opportunities there.

So I think you have to, again, being the data dog, you got to go sniffing in all the corners. Don't ignore a corner just because there's a legacy of it. Maybe just pull it apart, and see if there's opportunities.

Original Post

data.title: ISM Services PMI: Strongest Growth In 6 Months
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) released its August Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), with the headline composite index at 52.0, the highest level in six months. This was higher than the forecast of 50.9 and keeps the index in expansion territory for a third straight month.

Here is an excerpt from the report summary:

Miller continues, “August’s Services PMI® level showed greater strength, driven by faster expansion rates for the Business Activity and New Orders indexes. Offsetting these positive indicators, however, are continued contraction in the Employment Index, a 16-year low for the Backlog of Orders Index, and the Prices Index remaining near 70 percent. Commentary once again was led by respondents’ increasing citations of tariff impacts, with some indication that business activity and imports are being driven by an attempt to get ahead of additional price increases while preparing for the holiday peak season.”

Here is a table showing the PMI's components.

Unlike its much older kin, the ISM manufacturing series, there is relatively little history for ISM's non-manufacturing data, especially for the headline composite Index, which dates from 2008. The chart below shows the non-manufacturing composite.

The more interesting and useful sub-component is the non-manufacturing business activity Index. The latest data point for August is 55.0, the highest level in five months.

For a diffusion index, this can be an extremely volatile indicator, hence the addition of a six-month moving average to help us visualize the short-term trends.

Theoretically, this indicator should become more useful as the time frame of its coverage expands. Manufacturing may be a more sensitive barometer than non-manufacturing activity, but we are increasingly a services-oriented economy, which explains our intention to keep this series on the radar.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oscar Health: The Industry Is Having A Cold But 2026 Looks To Be Much Better
data.description:
Executive Summary

Oscar Health Inc. (OSCR) has grown significantly since its IPO in 2021 and was profitable in 2024. However, the company ran into some issues in 2025 but should be on the profitable path again in 2026. The technology platform is not only helping the company thrive but is also being sold to similar firms through +Oscar. The company is still heavily owned and run by its founders, Mario Schlosser and Joshua Kushner. The stocks for all the health insurance companies have taken a severe beating in 2025, and valuations are historically low. Oscar has built a decent cushion to survive through 2025 and is now a large company with $12 billion in revenue but only $4.3 billion in market capitalization. The company will increase its premium rate significantly for 2026, which is not just happening for OSCR, but an industry-wide phenomenon. The risk-reward seems very tilted in favor of substantial reward as the company turns profitable once again in 2026, and therefore, I’m initiating a Strong Buy.

Background

Oscar Health was founded in 2012 (was known as Mulberry Health Inc. and switched to Oscar Health in 2021) by Mario Schlosser, who was the CEO before hiring Mark Bertolini, and who is still the Chief Technology Officer; Kevin Nazemi, who has no role anymore; and Joshua Kushner, who was the largest financial backer by then and is still the largest owner. The guys were classmates at Harvard Business School and graduated in 2007. Oscar Health went public in March 2021, and the stock started trading on the NYSE under ticker OSCR at a price of $39/share. The valuation was $7.9 billion. The stock fell 10% on the first day and was down 94% at one point in 2023. The market cap is now around $4.3 billion at $17/share with 258.5 million A & B shares.

The company offers health insurance plans, predominantly through the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act, ACA, serving individuals, families, and employees. The company is technology-driven and not only offers support to its own insurance business but also provides technology to other providers and payors through +Oscar. Their platform, both online and mobile application, makes the onboarding and claims process more efficient, and according to its latest 10K, the company claims its business is “built upon our focus on member experience and our cloud-native technology platform.” The company had, as of June 20, 2025, over 2 million members and 2.9 billion in revenue for the last quarter. Oscar Health has its headquarters in Manhattan, NY.

Ownership and Governance

The company has 223 million Common A shares outstanding and 35.5 million Common B shares. The As are trading on the NYSE under ticker OSCR, but the Bs are not publicly traded. Class B is primarily held by the founders, notably Kushner through his firm Thrive Capital. The voting power is 20 to 1, but the same economics. After the IPO in 2021, Kushner owned 32.9 million shares of the B shares, and Schlosser owned 6.9 million B shares. At the end of Dec 2024, the founders owned, according to the 10K, 19.7% of the company and 79.9% of the voting power. Kushner is the majority owner and owns 17% of the company and 71.8% of the voting power; he holds 6.3 million A shares and most of the Common B. The largest owner of the A shares is Vanguard Group with 19.7 million A shares, or 7.6% of the company, followed by BlackRock with 16.6 million shares, or 6.4% of the company. The third largest is JP Morgan, with 13.8 million shares, or 5.3%. The next two largest hold both 11.8 million shares, or 4.6% of the company, and they are Deerfield Management Company and T. Rowe Price. The short interest is high with 62.5 million shares, or 24.2% of total shares outstanding, counting the B shares, or 28% excluding the B shares.

The company hired Mark Bertolini as the CEO in April 2023. He was the co-CEO of Bridgewater Associates, one of the largest hedge funds in the world. Before Bridgewater, he was the CEO of Aetna from 2010 until Aetna was sold to CVS in Nov 2018. Mario Schlosser had worked at Bridgewater and was able to get to know Mark before hiring him to take over his role as the CEO (from the 20VC podcast). The CFO is Scott Blackley since Aug 2023, and he was previously the CFO at Capital One (COF). And as mentioned, the co-founder Mario Schlosser has been the CTO since 2023, and he was the CEO before.

The board of directors consists of nine people. The chairman of the board is Jeff Boyd, former CEO at Priceline. He was a director of the board since 2015 and became chairman in 2021. The vice chairman is Joshua Kushner, co-founder since the start of the company in 2012. Mario Schlosser and Mark Bertolini are also on the board.

Business Model

The company is in the business of writing insurance premiums. They primarily offer individual health plans through health insurance marketplaces, established by the Affordable Care Act (ACA) and operated by the federal government, as well as by states. Individuals can also purchase policies off-exchange. Oscar is offering five different plans under categories defined by the ACA, which are, Catastrophic, Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum. The company is focused on a fully digitized, consumer-centric model and was investing early in technology and member engagement, and it has paid off with large member growth.

In general, an insurer needs to do four things. They need to acquire premium revenue, identify and try to reduce risk per member, authorize care, and manage the cost of care delivery. If you can master all four, you will have a successful business.

Oscar looks to have mastered acquiring premium as they have grown significantly in the last few years, through a modernized, user-friendly, and more customized approach. For instance, the company was first to offer $0 access to 24/7 telehealth for urgent care needs going back all the way to 2014. The company has also connected with its members, as 42% of its members are monthly users, through its digital app. The platform can also handle clinical and non-clinical conversations, and through its CRM tool, Rosco, it can guide members to meet their needs. The company’s tools are also good at figuring out the regulatory framework, and this specific technology should better identify and reduce risk per member.

The company’s development of specific insurance software has led them to sell the services to other insurance companies. However, this business is still only a small part of revenue.

Financial Performance

The company has grown substantially since the IPO in 2021 at a $7.9 billion valuation. Revenue was $1.9 billion in 2021 and is currently $10.5 billion LTM June 2025, and the company is guiding $12 billion for 2025. The last five-year CAGR, compounded annual growth rate, is 80.8%, and the last three-year CAGR is 53.2%. This growth has been achieved by growing members with ACA insurance plans. They had 578k members at the end of 2021, and at the end of 2Q25, they had over 2 million members. However, revenue and member growth have been expensive, as the company only became profitable in 2024. However, last quarter was a loss again, and the company is guiding for a loss for all of 2025 as well.

The company didn’t price its insurances appropriately for the medical loss rate experienced starting in the second quarter of 2025. However, the company believes resubmitting rate filings for 2026 in states covering approximately 98% of current membership to reflect the higher market risk scores in the ACA marketplace should lead the company to be profitable again in 2026. The medical loss rate went from 79% in 2Q24 to 91.1% in 2Q25 and was only 75.4% in 1Q25. This was the major reason for going from $275 million in net income in 1Q25 and $56 million in 2Q24 to a loss of $228 million in 2Q25.

On the positive side, SG&A as a percentage of revenue has come down as the company has been able to leverage its costs as they have grown. Last quarter the rate stood at 18.7% vs. 19.6% in 2Q24, and the company is guiding for it to reach 17.1% to 17.6% for 2025. As the company's price, its insurance policies, better for 2026, continues to attract more members due to its user-friendly process and leveraging its SG&A cost, losses should flip to profits again. There are some reports already that the average price increase for 2026 is close to 20%. The company should be able to achieve a 10% SG&A rate and 85% MLR, which means a 5% profit rate. That equals $600 million in net income on $12 billion in revenues.

Oscar Health has some revenues outside premiums, mostly from the +Oscar platform, but in relation it’s a smaller business. Last quarter the company made $6.5 million vs. the quarter year over year of $5.2 million. However, that’s not even 1% of total revenue.

The company was free cash flow positive in 2024 and built a solid liquidity cushion to sustain losses over the next couple of quarters. The parent company seems to be in good shape too when you read through the financials in the 10K and latest 10Q. As of June 2025, the parent company held $205.1 million in cash and investments, up from $189.8 million at the end of December 2024. The cash and investment amount at the various subsidiaries was $5208.9 million at the end of June 2025 and $3808 million at the end of December, and the combined statutory capital at the insurance level was estimated to be $1.2 billion and estimated to have $579 million of excess capital above the minimum requirement.

The company has a $305 million Convertible Senior Note due in 2031 paying a 7.25% rate, payable in semi-annual cash. It was a private placement issued in February 2022, and one of the holders is Thrive Capital (Joshua Kushner). The notes are convertible into 120.1721 shares per $1000 notes, equivalent to an initial conversion price of $8.32 per share of Class A common stock. The company also has a $115 million revolving credit facility with Wells Fargo due at the end of 2025 but has nothing drawn as of the end of June 2025.

Industry Issues

The health insurance industry in the US was $1.7 trillion in 2023, and according to Grand View Research will grow at 6% CAGR and be $2.5 trillion in 2030. The largest part is public, with the government providing insurance such as Medicare and Medicaid. Medicare, which is the largest, is a federal government scheme that offers health insurance for people aged 65 years or older, with around 55 million covered. The largest companies in the industry by written premiums are listed below, and the largest is UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and stock is down ~40% year to date. Other public companies are, Elevance Health (ELV), Centene Corp. (CNC), Humana (HUM), CVS Health (CVS), Cigna Group (CI), Molina Healthcare, (MOH). Most of those stocks are down double digits year to date. The list is from 2023, when OSCR “only” had $5.7 billion in premiums vs. now $10.5 billion and is guiding for $12 billion in 2025, which would have given them spot 17.

The Affordable Care Act (ACA), or Obamacare, is a comprehensive healthcare reform law that aims to make health insurance more affordable and accessible to more Americans. As of Feb 2024, there were 20.8 million enrolled with active policies and paid premiums. The uninsured has fallen drastically due to this program; about 14-16% of people in the years leading up to the law enacted in March 2010, were uninsured. The percentage by 2023 was a record low at 7.7%. The funding comes mainly from the government, as most people buying Obamacare health insurance get help paying for it in the form of subsidies from the federal government. People purchase these private health insurance plans through each state’s health insurance exchange. People who enroll in coverage through the exchange will either be directed to Medicaid or a private plan, depending on their income. Although Medicaid is a government-run program, it's typically administered by private insurance companies. So through this process, lower-income households might just get Medicaid-funded insurance. The enrollment process is only open from Nov 1st to Jan 15th and will take effect either Jan 1st, Feb 1st, or March 1st. If you end up getting Medicaid, it will start immediately. There is also a limited special enrollment period depending on your circumstances, like a change of marital status or birth of a child, then the coverage will take effect the month after you enroll.

In the new “One Big Beautiful Bill” signed into law in July 2025, there are some changes to who will be eligible for Medicaid and who will have access to ACA plans. There is a new “community engagement requirement,” which means people who want to continue to have coverage under Medicaid will have to verify 80 hours per month of working, volunteering, or attending school. There will be new requirements as well for people covered by the ACA plans, which are mostly in the renewal process, which won’t be automatic, and you will have to update info every year. The biggest change is the new law doesn’t extend the ACA premium tax credits, which are set to expire at the end of 2025, and would most likely increase the premium. This will certainly affect Oscar Health, and it is something to be concerned over. There is still a chance these tax credits might be extended. The state officials will most likely urge Congress to extend them.

Another issue for the industry is that healthcare costs in general have been on the rise, such as hospital expenses and prescription drugs.

Value Proposition

The value proposition for Oscar Health (OSCR) is the size of the premiums and the low market capitalization. The company is guiding for $12 billion in revenues for 2025, and with the rate increase for next year (2026), earnings should be at least 5% of revenues, or $600 million. The valuation seems too low with a $4.3 billion market capitalization.

The company is carrying a lot of cash, but most of it is for statutory purposes, and claimed excess cash was $579 million. The parent cash is $205 million, so if we use debt of $300 million (the convertible notes) minus parent cash of $205 million, the company's net debt is $95 million. The revenue growth in the last two quarters was 42% for 1Q25 and 29% for 2Q25. If we assume 20% revenue growth and turning profitable in 2026, one would be conservative in assuming a slightly less than market multiple of 1x sales and 20x EPS, which leads to ~$46/share at $12 billion in revenue and ~$53/share at $14 billion in revenue (20% topline growth in 2026).

Catalysts

The biggest catalyst coming is the changes in the Affordable Care Act, and they are mostly negative. However, the stock is probably pricing in the worst-case scenario, and it’s highly likely we will see an extension of tax credits to phase in. The other big catalyst, which is positive, is the rate increase for premiums charged in 2026, which should make the company profitable again.

Risks

The largest risk for the company is the concentration of ACA plan members. As mentioned, there are new rules that might make it harder to access ACA insurance. Another risk is continued losses in 2026/27 and negative free cash flow.

data.title: FDD: Caution Warranted Heading Into A Tough Seasonal Stretch
data.description:
The EURUSD currency pair hovers near its best level going back to 2021. That’s a boon for US investors owning ex-US shares and foreign ETFs denominated in dollars.

As such, we’ve seen tremendous alpha among many international market funds; specifically, European Financials stand out. I was also intrigued by a recent report from Goldman Sachs that pointed out strength in European value stocks.

One ETF, now ranked No. 1 in its Sub Class, per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, has seen a strong volume influx over the last handful of months.

The First Trust STOXX® European Select Dividend Index Fund (NYSEARCA:FDD) sports robust momentum and a low price-to-earnings ratio, but its earnings growth is questionable. Moreover, I’ll call out later in the article that the technicals are not all a green light.

EURUSD Holding Near Multi-Year Highs

GS: Value has outperformed in Europe, while growth has outperformed in the US

According to the issuer, FDD seeks investment results corresponding generally to the price and yield, before fees and expenses, of the STOXX Europe Select Dividend 30 Index. The Index is dividend-weighted, comprising 30 high-dividend-yielding stocks selected based on a positive five-year dividend-per-share growth rate, a dividend-to-earnings-per-share ratio of 60% or less, and an "outperformance factor" calculated from net dividend yield, with each component’s weight capped at 15%.

FDD is a small ETF with just $673 million in assets under management as of September 3, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 59 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 4.7%.

Share-price momentum has been stellar throughout 2025, earning the product a pristine A ETF Grade from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. The fund returned more than 50% from its early-year low to the 2025 peak. Today, however, some technical signals point to at least a consolidation. More on that to come.

In the here and now, FDD sports a decent ETF Risk Grade, thanks to its somewhat muted annualized volatility trend lately. I’ll caution prospective investors that its basket, heavy into European Financials, can be volatile when global growth worries perk up. Still, with average daily volume of more than 400,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of six basis points, liquidity is fine.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 4-star, Silver-rated ETF by Morningstar has very high exposure to both large-cap value and mid-cap value when scanning the style box. Just 14% of the allocation is classified as blend, and there is no growth access. That makes FDD is material factor wager on blue chips beating story stocks.

Value investors are likely intrigued by both the high yield and P/E ratio. But take a look at the long-term EPS growth rate of just 4.9%. The resulting PEG ratio is actually a bit pricey at more than 2.0x. So, I’m not excited about the growth-adjusted valuation.

FDD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, as hinted at earlier, FDD is significantly allocated to banks and other stocks from the cyclical-value Financials sector. That group has soared this year, thanks to GDP growth trends that are holding up in Europe, as well as the steepening yield curve trend seen across the Euro Area.

In terms of concentration, FDD’s top 10 holdings account for a notable, but not extreme, 41% of total assets. Moreover, I expect single-stock volatility to be somewhat modest now through mid-October (that’s when Financials-sector earnings reports begin to roll in).

FDD: Holdings & Dividend Information

FDD has recently garnered fanfare, but the product has been around since before the Great Financial Crisis (GFC). When scanning price-only returns, we find that the September-October period has been shaky. So, being prudent with an entry now through the early part of the fourth quarter could be a good move.

FDD: Weak Sept-Oct Trends

The Technical Take

With a valuation that’s not as attractive as might seem at first blush, FDD’s technical situation augurs for some caution. Notice in the chart below that shares are close to multi-year highs, but that the RSI momentum oscillator has printed a series of lower highs. That’s bearish divergence in the eyes of a technical analyst. What’s more, the fund is now probing its rising short-term 50-day moving average, so that bears watching.

But take a look at the long-term 200dma—it's on the rise, signaling that the bulls control the primary trend. Thus, the chart is by no means bearish. Additionally, with a high amount of volume by price (as noted by the horizontal bars on the left side of the graph), there should be ample support if we see an extended pullback. Overall, support is down at $15—I think that mark could get tagged later this month.

FDD: Bearish RSI Divergence, Rising 200dma, $15 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on FDD. This European dividend fund is heavily invested in Financials, while the valuation is not all that compelling on a growth-adjusted basis. Technicals, meanwhile, point to a continued retreat off the multi-year high.

data.title: IMTM: International Equities With Momentum
data.description:
Thesis

When the dollar is weak and weakening, an investor needs to look at assets denominated in other currencies. Those assets will rise in value as the dollar depreciates, all else equal. The most liquid international investments are exchange listed equities and fixed income. In today's article we are going to discuss an equities exchange traded fund from BlackRock, namely the iShares MSCI Intl Momentum Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:IMTM).

The U.S. dollar outlook is negative

The U.S. dollar has lost more than -9% this year:

The ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) is down -9.4% in 2025, and the outlook is not rosy:

Sept 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. dollar will weaken over coming months as market participants ponder the Federal Reserve's future independence and how many more rate cuts it may deliver, a Reuters survey of foreign exchange strategists showed on Wednesday.

The greenback, down nearly 10% against a basket of major currencies this year, has been the worst performer among them. The short-dollar trade has dominated FX markets since late March, according to Commodity Futures Trading Commission data.

Fundamental factors such as interest rates and deficits are driving the move in the dollar, thus further weakness should not be a surprise.

Holding international equities

Let us start with the objective of the IMTM fund:

The iShares MSCI Intl Momentum Factor ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index that measures the performance of international developed large- and mid-capitalization stocks exhibiting relatively higher momentum characteristics.

The followed index is the MSCI World ex USA Momentum Index, and the ETF ends up with the following geographic composition:

  • Germany: 15.2%
  • Canada: 15%
  • Japan: 14.4%
  • UK: 12%
  • France: 7%
  • Spain: 6.6%
  • Australia: 5%
  • Italy: 5%
  • Switzerland: 4%
  • Singapore: 2.7%

Please note the index and ETF do not contain any U.S. equities, and the geographic composition is fairly granular, lacking significant individual country concentration.

The top names in the fund are large capitalization stocks from very well-known issuers:

Although SAP (SAP) and Rolls-Royce (RR) are the top holdings (a technology company and an industrials one), from a sectoral standpoint Financials represent 41% of the ETF. Financials are followed by Industrials at 20%, and Communication at 7%. A severe global financial crisis would be the largest risk factor here given the sectoral break-down.

In addition to selecting international equities only, the index reflects the performance of an equity momentum strategy. It identifies companies that have shown strong price performance over the past 6 to 12 months, on the premise that this outperformance is likely to continue in the near term. The momentum factor thesis is built on the academic principle that stocks with high momentum tend to keep rising. A momentum score is calculated for each stock by combining its 6-month and 12-month price performance, adjusted for volatility. Subsequently a fixed number of stocks with the highest positive momentum scores are selected. The index is rebalanced on a semi-annual basis.

In the next section we will compare the IMTM performance with a simple international equities ETF, as well as look at the ETF's analytics.

Performance

Now that we have seen what the ETF holds and how it is constructed, let us see if the end results are comparable with a simple equities ETF, and if the analytics are favorable:

We are comparing IMTM to the Vanguard Developed Markets Index Fund (VEA), a simple international equities ETF that is widely used by market participants. In the past 3 years we can see a very close correlation between the two names, with a slight outperformance by IMTM in the past year. The net total return shows IMTM with a +10% outperformance.

On a longer timeframe we get the following annualized returns from Morningstar:

  • IMTM: 5-year 10.2%, 10-year 9.1%
  • VEA: 5-year 10.5%, 10-year 8.2%

VEA comes with a 16% 3-year standard deviation and a 0.74 Sharpe, while IMTM comes with a 15% 3-year standard deviation and a 0.95 Sharpe. The fund has indeed delivered superior returns to VEA, with a lower volatility profile as well. The analytics are therefore very good.

One has to be careful with momentum factor funds, because sometimes certain names produce better results but with much higher risk and volatility via high beta equities. It is not the case here.

Let us also have a look at the historic drawdown profile for the two names:

We can notice from the above chart that in most instances IMTM (the blue line) has lower drawdowns when compared to VEA (the red line). We can see that occurring in October 2022, in March 2023, in October 2023 and in April 2025. The only outlier is August 2024 when the one-day market meltdown saw a larger move down in IMTM when compared to VEA.

Valuations are still appealing

Although IMTM does not have a static portfolio, and its components change as their momentum factor changes, we are still going to have a look at valuations. As investors we would like to see if the ETF makes its selections and ends up with high beta components, or the holdings are within acceptable valuation levels. Courtesy of Morningstar we get the following table:

The portfolio P/E is at 16x, a very acceptable level when compared to the S&P 500 at 22x and the international equities index at 15.5x. Other valuation metrics also show levels which are in-line with overall markets, such as P/B (price to book) at 2x and P/S at 1.8x.

As it stands the fund's valuation metrics do not indicate high beta equity selection, just overall names with normalized valuations that have an upward momentum.

Conclusion

With the dollar set for further weakness, international equities are a good place to invest. IMTM adds a momentum factor on top of a portfolio of international equities (U.S. stocks are not allowed in the portfolio) and has managed to outperform the standard in the space (i.e. VEA) in the past three years. The ETF has achieved its metrics with a lower volatility profile than VEA and narrower drawdowns. We like this BlackRock product and its analytics, and view it favorable in a weaker dollar environment.

data.title: S&P Global Services PMI: Growth Remains Positive In August
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The August U.S. Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) from S&P Global came in at 54.5, below the 55.4 forecast. The reading marks the 31st consecutive month of expansion and the second strongest growth of 2025 so far.

From the latest press release, Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said:

“Although weaker than signaled by the preliminary ‘flash’ PMI reading, and below that seen in July, the expansion of the service sector in August was still the second strongest recorded so far this year. Together with a robust manufacturing PMI reading, the surveys are consistent with the US economy growing at a solid 2.4% annualized rate in the third quarter.

“Fuller order books, reflecting a summer upturn in customer demand, has meanwhile encouraged service providers to take on additional staff in increasing numbers, accompanied by a return to hiring in the manufacturing sector. While low household confidence is reportedly keeping spending on consumer services relatively subdued, demand for financial services is showing especially strong growth amid improving financial market conditions.

“However, the brighter news on current economic growth and hiring is marred by concerns over future growth prospects and inflation. Business optimism regarding the year ahead outlook has dropped to one of the lowest levels seen over the past three years amid escalating worries over the uncertainty and drop in demand caused by federal government policy, most notably tariffs, as well as the associated rise in price pressures. Inflation concerns have been fanned by a further steep rise in input costs which have fed through to another marked increase in average charges for services.

“The survey data therefore point to some downside risks to growth in the coming months while signaling upside risks to inflation, as import tariffs feed through to prices charged for both goods and services.”

Background on the S&P Global US Services PMI

The S&P Global US Services PMI™ measures the activity level of purchasing managers in the services sector through a questionnaire of ~400 service sector companies. The sectors covered include consumer (excluding retail), transportation, information, communication, finance, insurance, real estate, and business services.

The S&P Services PMI is a diffusion index, meaning that a reading above 50 indicates expansion in the sector compared to the previous month and a reading below 50 indicates contraction.

Here is a snapshot of the series since mid-2012.

Here is an overlay with the equivalent PMI survey conducted by the Institute for Supply Management, which it refers to as "non-manufacturing" (see our full article on this series here). Over its history, the ISM metric has been significantly more volatile of the two.

The next chart uses a three-month moving average of the two rather volatile series to facilitate our understanding of the current trend.

S&P Global PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
